games.explanation: Gavin Williams has a shiny ERA and a much less shiny walk rate, which is exactly the profile Washington's lefty-heavy top of the order is built to exploit. James Wood gets to hunt fastballs from a righty who hands out free passes to nearly 12% of the hitters he faces, and Abrams and García are patient enough to let Williams beat himself. Cleveland's edge here is supposed to be the arms, but Mikolas is a perfectly competent floor against a lineup leaning on Ramirez and two rookies still finding their footing. At plus money on a near coin flip, the board is paying you to bet against a name-brand starter who hasn't actually earned the discount.

Sanchez is a problem for left-handed bats and a much smaller problem for everyone else, which matters when San Diego runs a right-handed top of the order out there. Tatis, Machado, and Bogaerts all hit this exact profile harder than the slash line on Sanchez suggests, and his splits against righties drift toward the kind of contact quality that turns into runs in a hurry. Buehler is the obvious hole in the boat, but the market has priced this like Philadelphia is the better team by two touchdowns when the projected margin is a field goal. Getting +138 on a coin flip dressed up as a mismatch is the entire bet.

The market is pricing deGrom like the 2019 version, and Houston's lineup is built specifically to make that pricing look silly. Yordan, Peña, and Paredes are the kind of disciplined right-handed bats that drag deGrom into longer counts, and his xwOBA-against right-handers sits in territory that says he's good, not untouchable. Burrows is the obvious soft spot in the matchup, but Texas runs out a top of the order without a true thumper to exploit him, and the projected score lands inside a run. Getting a plus price on the better team in a coin-flip game is the whole exercise, and +138 is doing the heavy lifting.

picks.explanation: Gavin Williams has a shiny ERA and a much less shiny walk rate, which is exactly the profile Washington's lefty-heavy top of the order is built to exploit. At plus money on a near coin flip, the board is paying you to bet against a name-brand starter who hasn't actually earned the discount.

Gavin Williams gets treated like a frontline arm because the ERA is shiny, but the walk rate tells you he's one bad inning away from handing a game back at any moment. Washington runs out a left-handed-heavy order led by James Wood, and Williams has been issuing free passes to lefties at a double-digit clip all year. Cleveland's lineup without Bazzana and DeLauter contributing is basically Jose Ramirez and a prayer, which is not the profile of a team that blows anyone out. The sim sees a one-run game, the market is pricing a comfortable Guardians win, and getting a run and a half at a shade better than even money is the bet that falls out of that gap.
